description: Maps subcortical structures (Harvard-Oxford atlas) to the 10 HTB capability groups. Each region is assigned to the group whose combined cortical+subcortical circuit best aligns with the structure's role in learning and cognition.

groups.name: Strategic Thinking & Decision-Making
groups.rationale: The caudate nucleus drives goal-directed behavior and feedback-based learning — each outcome refines the next strategic choice through reward prediction error signals.

groups.name: Procedural Fluency & Muscle Memory
groups.rationale: The putamen encodes procedural/habit learning and motor sequence automation. The pallidum gates motor output, selecting which learned routines to execute. Together they convert conscious practice into unconscious skill.

groups.name: Technical Comprehension & Language Processing
groups.rationale: The thalamus (pulvinar and ventrolateral nuclei) relays and synchronizes cortico-thalamic language circuits, ensuring the right information reaches the right cortical area during complex comprehension.

groups.name: Situational Awareness & Focus
groups.rationale: The thalamic reticular nucleus and pulvinar form the brain's attentional gateway — filtering, gating, and routing sensory information to sustain focus and maintain situational awareness.

groups.name: Motivation & Adaptive Drive
groups.rationale: The nucleus accumbens is the brain's reward engine — its dopaminergic signals encode whether content is worth the effort, driving engagement, persistence, and the motivational state that sustains learning through difficulty.

groups.name: Memory Encoding & Knowledge Retention
groups.rationale: The hippocampus is the brain's memory writer — binding context, time, and content into durable episodic traces. High hippocampal engagement predicts content that will be retained long-term.

groups.name: Threat Awareness & Emotional Encoding
groups.rationale: The amygdala tags experiences with emotional significance — fear, urgency, excitement — which dramatically enhances memory consolidation. Content that triggers this circuit is remembered far longer than emotionally neutral material.
